entry.title: Bitcoin covenants are inevitable
entry.summary:
In the recent discussions surrounding the potential challenges that Bitcoin may face as block rewards diminish, a consensus appears to be forming around possible solutions that could ensure the continued security and viability of the network. It is suggested that if problems arise due to low block rewards, which are critical for maintaining network security, a variety of strategies including potentially hard forking the blockchain could be implemented. Such measures would aim to preserve some degree of block subsidy to indefinitely maintain acceptable levels of security.
Moreover, concerns about the complete failure of the system leading to total loss are deemed somewhat exaggerated. The resilience of blockchain technology lies in its ability to retain history; hence, even if the original chain becomes insecure, it is possible to move forward using this historical data as a basis for new, secure alternatives. This approach allows the socio-economic consensus to determine which version of the chain will be accepted moving forward.
The discussion also touches on more critical threats that could undermine the entire foundation of blockchain security, such as significant cryptographic advancements (e.g., quantum computing). Such breakthroughs might render traditional security measures obsolete, enabling threats like the transformation of unspent transaction outputs (utxos) into vulnerabilities that could be exploited by anyone. Despite potential chaotic transitions, similar to the "block size wars" of 2017, users have the option to "freeze" their utxos, thereby opting out of immediate changes and retaining their holdings on whatever emerges as the newly accepted chain.
